---
title: "Chapter 6: Drill planning under uncertainty"
subtitle: "Where should the next hole go? POMDP at regional scale + Gaussian process at property scale"
---

[← back to portfolio map](index.qmd)

## What this chapter is about

The earlier chapters build prospectivity maps. A prospectivity
map is a per-cell probability surface, and it doesn't tell you
where to drill *next* given what you've already drilled. Real
exploration campaigns are sequential: each hole costs money,
returns information, and that information should update the
next-hole decision.

This chapter has two pieces. The first is a POMDP planner with
Monte Carlo Tree Search (POMCP) on the BC Golden Triangle regional
prospectivity prior, asking where to sequentially drill within a
fixed budget. The second is a Gaussian-process drill-site
recommender on the Bear Cub placer property, asking where the next
hole should go given a sparse grid of 24 historical drill holes.

![POMCP discovery curves on the BC Golden Triangle prior. Each line is a different policy; the y-axis is the fraction of true deposits drilled after k holes. POMCP recovers more of the prior's mass per hole than uncertainty-sampling or random baselines.](data/derived/bcgt/fig_pomcp_discovery_curves.png)

## BCGT POMDP decision planning

The methodological reference is *Intelligent Prospector* v1.0 (Mern
et al., *Geosci. Model Dev.* 16:289, 2023). The setup:

- **State** = per-cell deposit indicator across a 30×30 working
  subarea (~15 km × 15 km centered on the BCGT prior's centroid
  of known occurrences). Cells are i.i.d. Bernoulli draws from
  the prior.
- **Observation** = noiseless reveal of the cell's true state
  when drilled. (v1.0 simplification; v2.0 adds measurement
  noise.)
- **Action** = drill cell (x, y), or stop and bank reward.
- **Reward** = $V per true positive drilled minus $C per drill.
- **Policy** = POMCP (Monte Carlo Tree Search over the belief
  MDP).

Three policies side-by-side on the same prior: random (uniform
over unrevealed cells), greedy (drill the highest-prior cell), and
POMCP. The POMCP planner gains roughly 10-15% in expected reward
over greedy at modest planner-depth budgets, and substantially
more at deeper budgets. The improvement is modest because the
BCGT prior is high-information already (much of the variance is
captured by the RF posterior), and because the v1.0
i.i.d.-Bernoulli + noiseless-sensor assumptions limit how much
MCTS rollouts can improve over a one-step-lookahead greedy. The
v2.0 version (correlated draws and noisy sensors) would widen the
gap.

## Bear Cub Gaussian-process drill-site recommender

The family's Bear Cub placer property has 24 historical drill
holes over a 597,000 ft² patent (see
[Chapter 1](bear_cub.qmd)). Given that sparse grid, where should
hole #25 go?

The model is a Gaussian-process regressor with an anisotropic RBF
kernel on (x, y) → predicted pay-zone grade. Two acquisition
functions:

- **Maximum predictive variance** picks the location maximizing
  the GP's posterior variance. The idea is to drill where the
  model is most uncertain, because that's where a new
  measurement teaches the model the most.
- **Expected improvement** picks the location maximizing
  E[max(grade(x) − target, 0)] under the GP posterior with
  target = 0.05 oz/yd³. The idea is to drill where the model
  thinks there might be a hit *and* it's not sure.

Both criteria exclude points within 80 ft of an existing hole.

![Recommended next-drill locations on the GP-predicted grade surface. Cyan triangles maximize predictive variance (info gain). Magenta stars maximize expected improvement against a 0.05 oz/yd³ target.](data/derived/bear_cub_resource/fig_gp_drill_recommender.png)

The two criteria recommend different locations: max-variance
picks the gaps in the existing drill grid (largest empty spaces
between holes), while expected-improvement picks gaps that are
also near already-known high-grade holes. For an exploration
program with a budget for one more hole, the EI criterion is what
most planners would use; for a research campaign focused on
understanding the deposit's overall shape, max-variance is more
informative.

**Limit.** The spatial-only GP doesn't predict pay-zone grade
accurately on n=24 holes. The leave-one-out R² is negative; the
model performs worse than just predicting the dataset mean. The
geological reason is that pay-zone grade has a sharp gradient at
the Third Beach Line crossing the property, and 24 spatial
samples can't constrain a gradient that steep. The recommender
output is **still meaningful even with a weak predictive model**:
it uses the GP's *uncertainty structure* (which is a faithful map
of where training data is sparse) rather than the GP's point
predictions. Max-variance picks remain the gaps in the grid;
EI picks remain gaps near known high-grade holes. The next hole
would help the GP fit the gradient.

## Where this goes from here

Both pieces here are early experiments at converting a probability
surface into a hole-location decision. The BCGT POMDP runs on a
regional prospectivity prior covering thousands of square
kilometers. The Bear Cub GP runs on 24 drill holes over a single
597,000 ft² patent. The underlying notebooks document the v1.0
simplifications I made, and the v2.0 extensions in the published
literature (Mern et al. 2023 for the POMDP) would tighten both.

## Deeper notebooks

- [Drill-planning chapter overview](https://johnsondevco.com/plans/ai-minerals-internal/notebooks/drill_planning/index.html): frames both pieces together.
- [BCGT POMDP decision planning](https://johnsondevco.com/plans/ai-minerals-internal/notebooks/bcgt/decision_planning.html): full notebook with POMCP implementation, random and greedy baselines, and the v1.0-vs-v2.0 limits discussion.
- [Bear Cub GP drill-site recommender](https://johnsondevco.com/plans/ai-minerals-internal/notebooks/drill_planning/bear_cub_gp_recommender.html): full notebook with both acquisition functions and the limits discussion.

[← back to portfolio map](index.qmd)
